title: Large Language Models for Scientific Idea Generation: A Creativity-Centered Survey
abstract: Scientific idea generation is central to discovery, requiring the joint satisfaction of novelty and scientific soundness. Unlike standard reasoning or general creative generation, scientific ideation is inherently open-ended and multi-objective, making its automation particularly challenging. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the generation of coherent and plausible scientific ideas, yet the nature and limits of their creative capabilities remain poorly understood. This survey provides a structured synthesis of methods for LLM-driven scientific ideation, focusing on how different approaches trade off novelty and scientific validity. We organize existing methods into five complementary families: External knowledge augmentation, Prompt-based distributional steering, Inference-time scaling, Multi-agent collaboration, and Parameter-level adaptation. To interpret their contributions, we adopt two complementary creativity frameworks: Boden taxonomy to characterize the expected level of creative novelty, and Rhodes 4Ps framework to analyze the aspects or sources of creativity emphasized by each method. By aligning methodological developments with cognitive creativity frameworks, this survey clarifies the evaluation landscape and identifies key challenges and directions for reliable and systematic LLM-based scientific discovery.

knowledge_gap.answer: The paper addresses the gap in understanding the creative capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in scientific idea generation, particularly the balance between novelty and scientific soundness.

answer: The paper organizes existing methods into five complementary families for LLM-driven scientific ideation.
It adopts two complementary creativity frameworks: Boden’s taxonomy and Rhodes’ 4Ps framework, to interpret contributions.
The survey clarifies the evaluation landscape and identifies key challenges and directions for reliable and systematic LLM-based scientific discovery.
evidence: We organize existing methods into five complementary families: External knowledge augmentation, Prompt-based distributional steering, Inference-time scaling, Multi-agent collaboration, and Parameter-level adaptation.

To interpret their contributions, we adopt two complementary creativity frameworks: Boden’s taxonomy to characterize the expected level of creative novelty, and Rhodes’ 4Ps framework to analyze the aspects or sources of creativity emphasized by each method.

By aligning methodological developments with cognitive creativity frameworks, this survey clarifies the evaluation landscape and identifies key challenges and directions for reliable and systematic LLM-based scientific discovery.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Wei et al. (2022) Techniques such as Chain-of-Thought prompting inspired structured reasoning methods. (Methodological precursors)
- Wang et al. (2023c) Self-Consistency decoding methods were used to enhance structured reasoning. (Methodological precursors)
- Yao et al. (2023) Tree-of-Thoughts methods were used to enhance structured reasoning. (Methodological precursors)
- Madaan et al. (2023) Iterative refinement methods like Self-Refine were used to enhance structured reasoning. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: Techniques such as Chain-of-Thought prompting (Wei et al., 2022), Self-Consistency decoding (Wang et al., 2023c), Tree-of-Thoughts (Yao et al., 2023), and iterative refinement methods like Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) enhance structured reasoning.

steps.output: Interpretation of contributions in terms of creative novelty and creativity sources.

methods.evidence: We outline open directions in Section 9. Current methods tend to remain at the combinatorial or exploratory level, with transformational creativity still elusive.

areas.name: Applied Sciences & Engineering
areas.description: The paper discusses methods for LLM-driven scientific ideation, which can be applied to engineering and technology fields.
areas.evidence: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer an unprecedented opportunity to augment this process.

areas.name: Social Sciences
areas.description: The paper explores creativity frameworks and cognitive dimensions, relevant to social sciences.
areas.evidence: In cognitive science, this ability is captured by creativity, which defines as requiring two jointly necessary components: novelty and valueness.

performance_summary.summary: The survey identifies key challenges and directions for reliable and systematic LLM-based scientific discovery.
performance_summary.evidence: By aligning methodological developments with cognitive creativity frameworks, this survey clarifies the evaluation landscape and identifies key challenges and directions for reliable and systematic LLM-based scientific discovery.

limitations.name: Limited Novelty Exploration
limitations.description: Efforts explicitly targeting novelty remain more limited and domain-dependent.
limitations.evidence: In contrast, efforts explicitly targeting novelty remain more limited and domain-dependent.

limitations.name: Reliance on Retrieved Content
limitations.description: Heavy reliance on retrieved content can bias ideas toward well-represented clusters.
limitations.evidence: Despite these advances, heavy reliance on retrieved content can bias ideas toward well-represented clusters, reinforcing conservative or incremental thinking rather than encouraging novelty.

future_directions.name: Explore Transformational Creativity
future_directions.description: Current methods tend to remain at the combinatorial or exploratory level, with transformational creativity still elusive.
future_directions.evidence: Current methods tend to remain at the combinatorial or exploratory level, with transformational creativity still elusive.

future_directions.name: Develop Standardized Metrics
future_directions.description: Address the lack of standardized metrics and benchmarks for evaluating generated scientific ideas.
future_directions.evidence: On the product side, the lack of standardized metrics and benchmarks for evaluating generated scientific ideas leaves creativity assessment vague and subjective.
